reason: Both answers correctly diagnose the Goodhart-style metric gaming, mean-vs-distribution issue, frequency/severity mix, and recommend SLO/error-budget burn plus percentile and total-impact-minutes measures, hitting all the key points. Each has one distinct, useful point the other lacks: A notes clock-start drift (mitigation-vs-fix, detection-delay creep) as a gaming mechanism; B notes survivorship/classification bias (chronic issues and near-misses that never become incidents, invisible to MTTR). These roughly offset. Both give the same actionable diagnostic (plot incident count vs total impact-minutes). Quality, correctness, and actionability are essentially equivalent.
